observations.recommendation:
We therefore reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of all the
inventories and properties; (ii) reconcile the results thereof with accounting
records; and (iii) submit the same to the Audit Team pursuant to Section
124, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs to allow the audit team to ascertain
the existence, validity and correctness of Inventories and PPE as well as the
reliability of balances presented in the financial statements.
b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) submit the subsidiary ledgers/supporting
schedules for inventory and PPE accounts to facilitate the verification of
balances presented in the financial statement; (ii) exert extra effort to trace
the details of unidentified PPE from previous years’ accounting records;
(iii) ensure that all PPE subject for depreciation have been provided with
depreciation expenses; (iv) effect the necessary adjustments to reclassify
tangible items below the capitalization threshold of P50,0000 to
appropriate accounts in accordance with COA Circular 2022-004; and (v)
submit a copy of Supplies/PPE Ledger Cards to the Audit Team for
verification, and:
c. Property/Supply Officer to maintain and regularly update the
Stock/Property Cards and periodically reconcile with accounting records.
We further recommended that the management ensure full
implementation of the guidelines/procedures set forth under COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the purpose of cleansing the enormous
amount of discrepancies in the PPE account balances of the municipality.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
exercise his authority to ensure that the Municipal Treasurer, COs, and deputized
BTs observe the pertinent laws, rules and regulation relative to cash management.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to establish strong internal controls in cash management by (i) ensuring
that all collections including those remitted by the collecting officers are timely
reported and deposited intact; (ii) directing all collectors to remit their collections
and prepare the RCD at the close of the business day or on the next working day; and
(iii) observing proper cut-off in the reporting of collections and submission of
report to the accounting office for proper recording.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant (i) effect the
necessary adjustments relative to reconciling items totaling P1,402,863.70; (ii)
prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the stale checks totaling P38,312.51 for
fair presentation and proper classification of account balances in the financial
statements; (iii) submit to the Audit Team the BRS with the corresponding bank
statements for the two bank accounts; and (iv) investigate the discrepancies noted
between the book balance and the balance per confirmation amounting to
P208,067.64 and to facilitate adjusting journal entries, if necessary.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (i) improve
coordination with payees to expedite presentment of checks for payment to avoid
stale checks; and (ii) observe the procedural guidelines on the cancellation and
replacement of stale checks prescribed in Section 59 of the Manual on the NGAS
for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. require all officers and employees who are still in the service to
settle/liquidate all their outstanding cash advances by sending demand
letters;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash
advances to any official or employee unless prior cash advance given was
first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section
4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and
c. explore available legal remedies under the one-time cleansing of dormant
accounts pursuant to COA Circular 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that the Municipal
Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. ensure that the RPT/SET Receivable as well as the related deferred income
accounts are set up at the beginning of every year based on the Real
Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card;
b. trace the discrepancies noted in the RPT/SET Receivables and their
corresponding Deferred Income accounts and prepare necessary adjusting
entries to correct their balances; and
c. observe proper recording of collections for advance payments, including
discounts granted as well as prior years’ taxes.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal
Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. conduct a thorough reconciliation between the balance of subsidiary
ledger/schedule, and the financial statement to determine the cause of the
discrepancy. Correct any recording error and ensure that adjusting
entries, if necessary, are duly supported and properly documented;
b. review the composition of the non-moving balances through available
records or by coordinating with the concerned source agencies. For
balances that are not supported with valid documents, initiate the
necessary actions to avail of the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008; and
c. coordinate with the respective source agencies to confirm the balances and
remit the unexpended balances of completed projects in compliance with
Items 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to (i) ensure that all transactions are properly recorded in their
appropriate account; and (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct
the erroneously recorded transactions so as to present reliable balance of Trust
Liabilities-DRRM account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Municipal Accountant to explore available legal remedies under the one-time
cleansing of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular 2023-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records with GSIS, Pag-IBIG and
PhilHealth and make the necessary adjustments and observe the
appropriate timing of recognition of transactions; and
b. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to identify the employees whose GSIS,
Pag-IBIG and Philhealth contributions and loan amortizations were still
not remitted and effect the immediate remittance thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the end-users to submit a
satisfactory explanation on the deficiencies noted in the preparation of ABC and
provide proof that they had conducted market research and/or analysis that
would support the amount presented in the ABC. Henceforth, require the end-
user to properly accomplish the ABC form and reflect the fair and reasonable cost
of items to be procured.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and
its TWG and Secretariat to:
a. scrutinize the documents submitted by the bidder and exercise diligence in
 the evaluation of bids, particularly in the post-qualification process during
which the responsiveness of the bids is being determined, and properly
disqualify the bidder if the documents are found to be lacking or deficient;
b. ensure that all necessary documents are complete before contract signing;
c. comply with the prescribed timelines in the posting of notices and
contracts in the PhilGEPS website to uphold transparency and accountability
in the procurement process; and
d. furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the PO and its attachments within
five working days from the issuance thereof as required under COA
Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and its TWG and
Secretariat to:
a. indicated the schedule of procurement activities in the APP;
b. scrutinize the documents submitted by the bidder and exercise diligence in
 the evaluation of bids, particularly the post-qualification process during
which the responsiveness of the bids is being determined, and properly
disqualify the bidder in the event that the documents are found to be
lacking or deficient;
c. ensure that all necessary documents are complete before contract signing;
d. comply with the prescribe timelines in the posting of notices and contracts
 to the PhilGEPS website to uphold transparency and accountability in the
procurement process; and
e. furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contracts and its attachments
within five working days from the issuance thereof as required under COA
Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) deduct 10 percent retention, secure warranty in
accordance with the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and secure
the CARI from the contractor to ensure that the Municipality is adequately
protected against any potential risks during project implementation; and (ii)
ensure the completeness and accuracy of supporting documents before
processing claims;
b. Supply Officer or the Inspectorate Officer to properly accomplish the AIR
reflecting the actual description of items received from the supplier;
c. concerned personnel for the Supply and Delivery of One Unit Quick Response
Vehicle and Supply and Delivery of 23 Cattle Brahman (Heifer) to submit
satisfactory justification on the noted deficiencies; and
d. Municipal Engineer to properly evaluate the claims of the contractor and
ensure that projects are implemented according to approved scope and
specifications.
We also recommended that the Municipality consider the formulation of a clear-
cut policy on how to strengthen the monitoring of the implementation of projects,
determine accountability over the delayed implementation of projects as well as the lack
of concrete action to enforce the terms and conditions of the contract and consider the
imposition of sanctions to those who committed neglect and inefficiency in the
performance of official duties.

We reiterated our recommendations with some revisions that the Municipal
Mayor:
a. enjoin the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (i) reconcile
the balances of the CLT account in their respective reports to reflect
accurate and reliable balance appearing in the FS; and (ii) cancel the stale
checks amounting to ₱719,316.49, revert the same to the payable account,
and ensure compliance with the procedural guidelines on the cancellation
and replacement of stale checks prescribed in the Manual on the NGAS for
LGUs, Volume I to arrive at the accurate CIB balance per books; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure that the CIB balance
reflected in the unadjusted book balance portion of BRS is reconciled with
the amounts in the SL, to arrive in an accurate amount of adjusted cash
balance; (ii) thoroughly review the preparation of BRS and make sure that
all reconciling items are well recorded and properly posted in the SL, in
order to arrive at the accurate amount of CIB-LCCA account to be reported
in the FS; (iii) identify the nature of the bank reconciling items amounting
to ₱718,445.38 and make a representation to the bank, ensuring that
corrections/adjustments will be effected for the total bank reconciling items
of ₱718,805.38; (iv) reclassify the savings account under Bank Account No.
00-5-00379-606-0 from CIB-LCCA account to CIB-SA account; and (v)
make sure that all bank statements attached to the BRS are stamped as
received by the MAO so that determination of delay in the preparation and
submission of BRS could be established.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (i) include in the RUCA and SUCA all outstanding cash
advances and reconcile the same with the balances reflected in the FS; (ii)
update the individual SLs by posting the liquidated cash advances of
₱10,818,000.00, review the abnormal balance of ₱2,018,992.86 and draw
adjustments, if warranted, and record the adjustments with net amount of
₱3,890.00 in the SL of the respective AOs; (iii) prepare adjusting entries to
reclassify the cash advances valued at ₱4,182,515.00 to their appropriate
accounts pursuant to Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009; (iv) demand
from AOs who are still in service, the immediate liquidation of their
outstanding cash advances. Failure on the part of AOs to
settle/liquidate their cash advances shall constitute a valid ground for the
withholding of the payment from any money due them; (v) exhaust all
remedies to collect long outstanding cash advances of individuals who are
already deceased, separated from service, and had transferred to other
government agencies by sending confirmation, notification, or demand
letters, otherwise, apply for write-off in accordance with COA Circular No.
2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and (vi) refrain from granting additional
cash advances to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance
has already been settled.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the MGSO to (i) prepare the SSMI on a weekly basis and provide
the same report to the MAO; and (ii) prepare the SCs for all inventory
accounts, to ensure that receipt and issuance of inventory items are properly
accounted; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) observe timely preparation of JEV
for the issuance of inventory items and ensure that inventory movements
are properly and accurately recorded in the GL so that balances are fairly
presented in the FS; and (ii) prepare the SLCs for all inventory accounts, to
ensure that the recording of the acquisition, issuance and disposal of
inventories are well monitored and documented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. MGSO to (i) prepare and maintain the PCs for each type of PPE and
indicate the complete details and assigned property number to each PPE;
(ii) reconcile the RPCPPE with the PCs and ensure the timely submission
of the report to the Office of the Auditor; (iii) coordinate with the MAO for
the reconciliation of their reports and make the necessary adjustments, if
warranted; (iv) cancel the PARs issued to officials/employees who are no
longer connected with the Municipality and ensure that these are updated
every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of
the property; and (v) report all the unserviceable properties in the IIRUP,
properly supported with complete documentation for the subsequent
disposal activities in accordance with COA rules and regulations; and
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain complete property
records such as PPELC for each type of PPE; (ii) provide the GL with
complete and detailed SLs, indicating the complete details such as the
property number assigned to each PPE and the breakdown of the
unidentified PPEs; and (iii) prepare necessary adjusting entries to reclassify
the semi-expendable properties amounting to ₱1,406,964.00, misclassified
ICT equipment of ₱1,683,395.00, and overstated depreciation expense of
₱676,333.33.

We reiterated our recommendations with some revisions that the Municipal
Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) individually verify and validate the
SL account balances of the Due to NGAs account, provide with complete
description and significant detail of transactions recorded therein and ensure to
substantiate with valid supporting documents; (ii) review the erroneous recording
and posting of transactions in the books of accounts, analyze and identify the cause
of negative balances in the SLs, and effect the necessary adjustment, if warranted;
(iii) submit the FURs for the disbursements/remittances amounting to
₱893,268.06, in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013; and (iv) remit the
collections received of ₱763,363.06 to concerned government agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG to (i) submit written
justification/explanation on the noted lapses and deficiencies; (ii) submit all
lacking bid documents to the Audit Team for evaluation; and (iii) furnish
the Audit Team with copy of the perfected contracts and its attachments
within five wd from execution thereof as required under Section 3.1.1 of
COA Circular No. 2009-001;
b. instruct the Municipal Engineer to (i) incorporate accurate and complete
details in the preparation of the STE; and (ii) review thoroughly the
submitted SWA of the contractor prior to its approval; and
c. direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) submit to the Audit Team the noted
lacking documents for verification; (ii) check the completeness and veracity
of supporting documents prior to processing of claims in compliance with
COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445; and (iii)
ensure accurate computation of the withholding taxes before making
payments to contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the BAC to (i) submit written justification/explanation on the above
noted lapses and deficiencies; (ii) submit all lacking bid documents to the
Audit Team for evaluation; and (iii) furnish the Audit Team with the copy
of the POs and its attachments within five wd from issuance thereof as
required under Section 3.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to check the completeness and veracity of
supporting documents prior to processing of claims in compliance with
COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) require the concerned contractor to immediately correct and rectify the
noted deficiency on the two projects to conform with the approved POW,
or in case of failure to satisfactorily rectify the noted defects, initiate the
institution of appropriate legal actions against the erring contractors; and
b) instruct the Municipal Engineer to (i) provide acceptable justification with
regard to the COA-TAG observation that the ABC and contract costs of the
project Potable Water System at Barangay Lonos, Romblon, Romblon was
above the COA Cost Estimates; and (ii) direct the Municipal Engineer to
submit immediately to the Audit Team the copies of PERT/CPM and S-
Curve/Bar Chart, reflecting the planned/actual project accomplishments
per month, to facilitate further evaluation of the COA-TAG and
computation of the corresponding liquidated damages.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) exert efforts in determining the
specific details of the reconciling items totaling to P4,637,156.49 and draw
the adjusting entries therefor; and (ii) maintain SL for each depository
account of the Municipality; and
b. direct the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to expedite the updating of the
cashbooks for Cash in Bank and provide the Audit Team copy thereof for
review.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct
the balance of the accounts affected by the improper recording of
collections, deposits and prior period errors; and
b. ICO-Municipal Treasurer to (i) immediately deposit all collections with the
authorized depository bank and ensure that all collections are remitted and
deposited intact and promptly; (ii) ensure proper reconciliation of the CLT
cashbooks with the accounting records; and (iii) submit to the Audit Team
the updated cashbooks.

Based on the foregoing audit observations, we reiterate our previous
recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) be cautious in recording the
financial transactions of the Municipality and ensure the proper use of
accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 in recording
financial transactions; (ii) analyze the details of the discrepancies noted in
Annex F and prepare the necessary adjusting entries; and (iii) prepare and
maintain SL per employee for each cash advance account;
b. require all accountable officers and employees to immediately settle their
outstanding cash advances and henceforth, liquidate their cash advances
within the prescribed period provided in Item 5 of COA Circular No. 97-
002; and
c. exert extra efforts to recover outstanding cash advances from Accountable
Officers who are no longer in the service nor connected with the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to look into the cause of the variance amounting to P1,446,290.80 and
prepare the necessary adjusting entries.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) prepare the adjusting entries on the noted
errors in observations (a) and (b) to reflect the correct balances of each
affected account; and (ii) maintain subsidiary ledger for each fund
transfer received to readily determine information on each fund and easily
track the transactions and movements including related cash and payables;
and
b. ICO-Municipal Treasurer to remit to the Bureau of the Treasury
collections of electrification fees pertaining to the share of the national
government.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) be guided by COA
Circular No. 2015-009 which provide for the Revised Chart of Accounts for the
LGUs in the recording of transactions; and (b) draw the necessary adjusting
journal entries to present the correct balance of the accounts affected.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the designated Supply Officer to use the RPCPPE in reporting the
results of the physical inventory of Municipality’s PPEs;
b. instruct the Municipal Inventory Committee to undertake all other procedures
prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to come up with the reconciled
and correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements;
c. direct the designated Supply Officer and Municipal Accountant to prepare
and maintain the PC and PPELC, respectively, for each item of the PPE
pursuant to Section 114, Paragraph 4 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
d. require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entries for the
errors noted in observation c.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. ICO-Municipal Treasurer to henceforth prepare and submit the List of
Taxpayers with their corresponding amount due and collectible for the year to the
Municipal Accountant; and
b. Municipal Accountant to set up the estimated RPT and SET Receivable and
the corresponding Deferred RPT/SET at the beginning of the year based on the
List of Taxpayers to be submitted by the ICO-Municipal Treasurer.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the necessary schedule/aging of the
above-mentioned payable accounts as required under Section 3.0 of COA
Accounting Circular Letter No. 2007-002 to the Audit Team for verification and
validation of the balances reported in the financial statements.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to provide disclosures of the reconciliation of actual amounts per
SCBAA and per FS on the face of the SCBAA or in the Notes to FS to enhance the
reliability of the financial statements.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. ICO-MPDC to (i) henceforth ensure that all identified projects to be
implemented under the DF are incorporated in the AIP; (ii) see to it that
the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation-ready to avoid spillovers in the
succeeding year pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1;
(iii) closely monitor the implementation of development projects and
ensure that these are promptly and timely implemented for the benefit of
the constituents; and (iv) consult with professionals for the planning and
implementation of highly technical projects; and
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) see to it that all the necessary documentary
requirements attached to the vouchers are accurate and complete before
processing the claims; and (ii) properly compute the taxes to be withheld
from the payee.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor instruct the:
a. OIC-MPDC to incorporate in the AIP the PPAS listed in the LDRRMFIP;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) comply with the accounting and reporting
guidelines for LDRRM Fund set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-002;
(ii) review the regularity and completeness of supporting documents of DVs
prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing claims for
payment; (iii) submit the aforementioned lacking documents to the Audit
Team to prove the transaction’s validity, accuracy, regularity and propriety;
(iv) transfer the unexpended balance of LDRRMF for CY 2024 to the STF as
prescribed under Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and (v) ensure
that details of the unexpended balance of the Municipality’s LDRRMF for the
current year as well as that of the previous years are properly disclosed in the
Notes to Financial Statements.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Members of the GFPS to prepare the Municipality’s GAD Agenda and
furnish a copy to the Audit Team;
b. Chairperson, GPFS-Technical Working Group (TWG)/GAD Focal
Person to prepare a revised GPB, if necessary, to ensure conformance of
the GAD Budget per GPB with the budget allocation in the approved
Annual Budget;
c. Bids and Awards Committee and its Secretariat to be guided by COA
Circular No. 2009-001 in the submission of contract/purchase order and
the provisions of R.A. No. 12009 in the procurement of goods and services;
and
d. Municipal Accountant to (i) check the completeness and veracity of
supporting documents prior to processing of claims in compliance with
Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445; and (ii) adhere strictly to the requirements
of Executive Oder No. 77 in processing the claims for travel of LGU
officials and employees.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (i) facilitate the
immediate settlement of the overdue unremitted balances to the concerned
government agencies; and (ii) henceforth ensure that the full amount of taxes
withheld and mandatory contributions deducted are remitted intact to the
concerned government agencies within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) submit to the Audit Team
the transmittal letter of the DV and supporting documents pertaining to the cash
advance liquidated as required under Section 6.2.3.6 of the JC; (ii) submit to the
ICFAU the LR of the cash advance granted to him for confidential expenses; and
(iii) instruct the Municipal Accountant to henceforth record the grant and
liquidation of cash advance for CF under Advances to Special Disbursing Officer
account.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require
the BAC to (i) explain in writing the above noted lapses and deficiencies;
(ii) enhance internal controls over post-qualification; and (iii) provide the Audit
Team with a copy of the Contract Agreement and its attachments within five cds
from execution, as mandated by Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.
For procurements made after the effectivity date (February 25, 2025) of the IRR
of R.A. No. 12009, or the New Government Procurement Act (NGPA), the
management is reminded to strictly adhere to its provisions to ensure adherence to the
principles of transparency, competitiveness, efficiency, proportionality, accountability,
public monitoring, procurement professionalization, and sustainability in government
procurement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the persons liable to settle immediately the audit disallowances
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006. For those who are retiring after
the issuance of the Notices of Finality of Decision (NFD), strictly require an
Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities prior to
processing of their claims for terminal pay in order that their
accountabilities are settled. For those who are no longer connected or
working with the local government unit prior to the issuance of the NFD,
direct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters for the full
settlement of their accountabilities.
The Municipality is apprised of Section 16.3 of the 2009 RRSA which
provides that the liability of persons determined to be liable under an ND
shall be solidary and the Commission may go against any person liable
without prejudice to the latters claim against the rest of the persons liable.
Underscoring ours; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to support recorded disallowances with
subsidiary ledgers/records containing proper disclosure on the reference
NFD, persons liable, and amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor demand the six former
Collecting Officers to turn over the accountable forms mentioned above.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) and the Municipal Budget Officer

 revert the salary rates to previous monthly basic salary in order to reduce the PS cost and comply with the PS limitation requirement; and


 direct the refund of the salary increase (net of withholdings made).
observations.managementAction:
The management constantly reminded the concerned offices on these audit findings of the LGU.




No actions taken for this audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
Audit observation for CY 2021 revealed that instead of reverting the rates of monthly basic salary to previous year’s pay, the Municipality rather implemented another salary adjustment despite of the PS excess case of the LGU.

Notice of disallowance (ND) on this audit recommendation has been served to the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

 draw the necessary adjusting entry for the noted misstatement; and



 observe properly the correct usage of intra-agency accounts and make sure that they are always equal.
observations.managementAction:
No JEV was provided to the Audit Team.




No action taken for this audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
We have verified the details posted in the GL of the affected accounts and we noted that no adjustment was made for the discovered misstatement in CY 2019.

Still, the balances of intra-agency accounts did not tally as at December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

 make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the payments made for the quarterly amortization P903,970.15.
observations.managementAction: No JEV was provided to the Audit Team.
observations.reason:
The Audit Team could not validate the adjusting entries drawn by the Accounting Office. 

Moreover, no SL and GL was provided by the Accounting Office for the said account.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

 ensure that the transfer of unutilized/

unexpended DRRMF is recorded on time by debiting the Transfer of Unspent Current Year DRRMF to the Trust Fund 
(5-02-15-010); 


 consider the presented PAJE in order to correct the Due from Other Funds account balance and present the affected accounts fairly in the financial statements; and


 reconcile the reciprocal accounts of Due from/to Other Funds.
observations.managementAction:
No action taken for the said audit recommendation.






The Accounting Office claimed that they have already adjusted the misstatement discovered in prior year to reflect the correct account balance.




No action for the said audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
It has been learned that the practice of Municipal Accountant on the transfer of all unutilized balance of current year’s MF - MOOE and QRF are being effected for the ensuing year taking up as a prior period adjustment.

The Accounting Office still did not furnish the Audit Team with copies of journal entry voucher (JEV) for the said adjustments thus, we could not validate such entries drawn by them.

Verification of the CY 2021 FS revealed that the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from/to Other Funds were still not tallied.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

  instruct the Municipal Accountant to accurately and completely record the transactions in the GL and consider the PAJE of the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: No JEV was provided to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The Audit Team still noted that the remaining balance as at year-end is composed of unadjusted balance from prior years’ unreconciled withholdings and remittances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

  require the Municipal Engineer to explain/justify the higher ABC and contract amount over the cost estimate per audit for the project Design, Build and Supply                of -20ºC Cold Storage Facilities and Equipment and to submit to the Audit Team for further evaluation the Detailed Breakdown of ABC, complete set of plans and drawings, variation order and design specifications for the 67.62% of the total works and the documents supporting the request for time extension; 

 require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to explain/justify the observations on quorum, arithmetical error, similar amounts and the same date of Program of Work (POW) preparation and bidding activity; and 

  explain/justify why the project has not been utilized and non-operational for years since its completion and apprise the Audit Team of its current operational status.
observations.managementAction:
The Engineering Office submitted on May 18, 2020 the POW and the contractor’s Detailed Cost Estimate, set of plans and drawings and documents to support the request for time extension.




No information was provided to the Audit Team.





Management updated the Audit Team that the project was still non-operational.
observations.reason:
The Engineering Office did not explain/justify the difference between the ABC and contract amount versus the cost estimate per audit for the project Design, Build and Supply of -20ºC Cold Storage Facilities and Equipment.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
instruct the Inventory Committee/Property Custodian to (i) conduct complete physical count of all the Inventories and PPE of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding inventory reports; (ii) reconcile the results of the count with the property and accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team; and (iii) report all the unserviceable properties in the IIRUP supported with proper documentation for subsequent disposal of the same in accordance with COA rules and regulations and (iv) strictly comply with the guidelines/procedures set forth under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the purpose of cleansing the enormous amount of discrepancies in the PPE account balances of the Municipality; and
require the Municipal Accountant to (i) provide allowance for depreciation for PPE totaling ₱74,757,296.90 and prepare necessary journal entries to record the depreciation pursuant to IPSAS No. 17; (ii) review the PPE account balances and draw journal entries to effect the necessary adjustments to correct the misclassified accounts and reclassify tangible items below the capitalization threshold of ₱15,000.00 in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; and (iii) effect the prompt transfer of the cost of the completed projects from the Trust Fund to the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant:
to establish the composition or breakdown of the balance of Cash in Bank –LCCA accounts in all Funds and, thereafter, reconcile with the SL balances to ensure accurate and reliable information as to the details of the balances of the controlling account appearing in the GL and to reconcile the same with the balances appearing in the Cashbooks maintained by the Cashier;
to investigate and determine what caused the variance of balances in the SL against the unadjusted book balance in the bank reconciliation statements; and
to prepare a comprehensive list of all valid book reconciling items and adjust them accordingly in the book of accounts and present fairly the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to  (i) make necessary adjustment for errors identified and for the unrecorded fund transfer of ₱2,314,187.00; (ii) debit the Due to NGAs account only for completed PPAs and discontinue the practice of debiting the Due to NGAs account for the payment of the first and subsequent progress billings to avoid understatement of the same account; (iii) substantiate the nine adjusting journal entries with supporting documents, schedules, computations or analysis, copy furnished the audit team, in order to attain fair presentation of the Due to NGAs account in the Financial Statement; and (iv) prepare schedule of fund transfers received from various NGAs for timely monitoring of receipts, liquidation and return/refund of any unutilized amount and submit the same to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to see to it that the net take-home pay of employees shall not be less than the mandatory minimum amount of ₱5,000.00. In the event that the total authorized deductions shall reduce the net take-home pay to less than the minimum amount, deductions shall be made in accordance with the order of preference under Section 49 of the GAA for FY 2021.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
institutionalize the standard procedures in granting AICS and other financial assistance based on DSWD MC No. 11, s. 2019 as amended by MC No. 24,             s. 2020 and ensure that beneficiaries undergo screening and assessment by designated DSWD staff;
refrain from making payments on reimbursement basis, instead, require the Municipal Treasurer to make the check payable to the client/recipient of the beneficiary pursuant to Section 93 of P.D. No. 1445; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure that all claims for financial assistance be supported with complete and proper documentation pursuant to Section 4.6 of P.D. No. 1445, to avoid possible disallowance in audit and  (ii) record properly the release of financial assistance to AICS in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
the BAC to submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents on the above-mentioned procurement; and
the Municipal Accountant to (i) submit all lacking/missing documents relative to the above procurement of COVID-19 related PPAs and various goods; and (ii) impose and collect the appropriate liquidated damages           (Annex L) pursuant to Item 3.1, Annex “D” of the Revised IRR.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
enjoin the LDRRMO and the Municipal Budget Officer to (i) review thoroughly the appropriations under LDRRMF, and henceforth, ensure that the LDRRMFIP is properly integrated in the Annual Budget and AIP; and  (ii) together with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and corresponding reports to ensure consistency and accuracy of balances;
instruct the LDRRMO to (i) maximize the utilization of LDRRMF and make sure that the identified priority projects will be implemented during the year, and henceforth, closely monitor the implementation thereof to deliver satisfactory benefits to the constituents; and (ii) instruct the LDRRMO to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and
require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended/unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund as at year-end, and disclose the same in the Notes to FS including those pertaining to prior years in compliance with the above-mentioned regulations and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the Trust Liability – DRRMF account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
instruct the Municipal Engineer and MPDC to (i) implement the projects funded by the 20 percent Development Fund for CY 2021 including those PPAs funded through realignments and update the Audit Team as to their status; and (ii) always disclose all government infrastructure projects in the Quarterly Report of Government PPAs and submit the same to the Audit Team as required under Item 2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
direct the MPDC to (i) ensure that all the development projects to be funded out of the 20 percent DF are well-planned and procurement-and implementation-ready as required under Item 3.2.3 of above-mentioned DBM-DOF-DILG JMC and refrain from inputting lump sum or generic term appropriation as it will need separate approval from the sanggunian; and (ii) provide the Audit Team with copies of SB Resolutions covering the PPAs that had been identified by the 28 barangays and funded out by the  CY 2021 lump-sum or generic term appropriation for the Support to Infrastructure Projects to 31 Barangays.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the GFPS and Municipal Budget Officer to review thoroughly the appropriations under GAD and ensure that the GPB is properly integrated in the Annual Budget to ensure that GAD PPAs are adequately funded.
However, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort to investigate the unreconciled balance of the Due to BIR account and effect adjusting entry/ies, if warranted.

1.20 We recommended that the Provincial Accountant (i) coordinate with the
Provincial Treasurer in identifying the details of the reconciling items
reflected in the BRS and effect the necessary adjustments, if warranted; (ii)
prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the unreleased checks totaling
P9,210,370.38 and stale checks totaling P37,623.63 for fair presentation and
proper account classification of balances in the financial statements; and (iii)
submit to the Audit Team the BRS with the corresponding bank statements
for the time deposit accounts.
1.21 Likewise, we recommended that the Provincial Treasurer (i) improve
coordination with payees to expedite releasing of checks and to avoid
accumulation of unreleased and outstanding checks; and (ii) observe the
procedural guidelines on the cancellation and replacement of stale checks
prescribed in Section 59 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs-Volume I.

2.11 Considering the lapse of time that these accounts remained outstanding, we
reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the Provincial
Governor:
a. For cash advances of accountable officers who are still connected
with the agency, require the Provincial Accountant to take extra
effort to demand the immediate settlement of outstanding cash
advances, particularly the outstanding balances since December 31,
2011 from the concerned officials and employees pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 and COA Circular
No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
b. For unsettled accounts of personnel who are no longer connected
with the agency, issue demand letters to enforce settlement of the
outstanding cash advances and if warranted, file a request for
write-off to the Commission on Audit, particularly for the cash
advances of those Accountable Officers who are already deceased
and settlement of which is already impossible, in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
c. Require the Provincial Legal Officer to initiate the imposition of
sanctions or file appropriate legal actions against the erring
accountable officers pursuant to Item 9.3 of COA Circular No.
2012-004.

3.32 With the foregoing, we recommended that the Provincial Governor:
a. Constitute the Inventory Committee and require them to prepare a
Physical Inventory Plan pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020 and conduct a complete physical count, semestrally for
inventories and annually for PPE. Subsequently, reconcile the results of
46
the count with the accounting records, identify the discrepancies and
record the necessary adjustments thereon and submit the complete RPCI
and RPCPPE to the Audit Team;
b. Require the Accounting Office and GSO to maintain the prescribed
property records and ensure that these records are regularly reconciled
to achieve a reliable financial reports/statements;
c. Direct the Provincial Accountant to (i) re-evaluate the designation and
assignment of accounting staff and ensure that respective duties and
functions are religiously performed particularly on the recording of PPE
accounts; (ii) provide depreciation for all the depreciable assets totaling
P396,417,636.23 pursuant to IPSAS No. 17; (iii) always exercise prudence
in recording the transactions considering the substance over form; (iv)
reclassify from the Land account the identified land improvements and
survey expenses of P225,214.56 to reflect the correct PPE account
balances, pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs; and (v)
transfer to the General Fund the costs of the completed projects of
P40,211,037.28 which are still recorded under the Trust Fund books; and
d. Require the Provincial Assessor in coordination with other concerned
officials to immediately work on the titling of the parcels of land owned
by the Province amounting to P14,740,885.19.

4.9 We recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the personnel in-
charge to:
a. Regularly reconcile records with GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth and
identify those employees who have been dismissed from their office and
those who already reached the age of mandatory retirement exclude
those in active employees service to facilitate reconciliation and make the
necessary adjustments, if any;
b. Identify the employees whose GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth
contributions or loan amortizations were still not remitted and effect the
immediate remittance thereof;
c. Continue to reconcile the employees’ records with changes to facilitate
full remittance of the GSIS premiums and loan amortizations; and
d. Fully remit withheld amounts within the reglementary period provided
by laws and regulations.

5.31 We recommended that the Provincial Accountant (a) maintain sufficient
records/details of the accounts under the TF and perform periodic
verification, analysis and validation of the balances of the fund transfers to
ensure the implementation of projects in accordance with their purpose and
objectives; (b) exert vigorous efforts to determine the details of the Due to
NGAs account and confirm the correctness of the balance of the long-
outstanding and unliquidated fund transfers by sending confirmation letters,
coordinating with the source agencies, and returning the unutilized balances,
if warranted; (c) submit duly certified Fund Utilization Report together with
proof of utilization and/or project completion for the liquidation totaling
P326,962,828.29; (d) revert to the respective accounts the equivalent amounts
written-off of P10,272,172.14 and file a request for authority to write-off
dormant fund transfers to the COA after the conditions and requirements
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 have been fully satisfied; (e) review
the status of implementation of the projects funded out of the fund transfers
and make necessary adjustments for the completed infrastructure projects;
and (f) return the unexpended balances of completed projects to the source
agencies in compliance with Item 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated
December 13, 1994.
5.32 Further, we recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the
respective Project Implementing Offices to regularly monitor and expedite
the implementation of the projects under the trust fund to ensure its prompt
liquidation. Likewise, require each contractor to expedite the completion of
the unfinished/on-going projects and submit claims right after for timely
liquidation.

6.5 We recommended that the Provincial Accountant reclassify the balance
totaling P25,518,862.35 from the Due to NGAs account to Due to GOCCs to
facilitate proper presentation and disclosure in the FS in compliance with
IPSAS 1 - Presentation of FS and the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.

7.7 We recommended that the Provincial Governor, thru the Implementing
Units, submit justification and/or necessary documents for the authorization
on the use of fund for purpose other than its intended purpose. Further,
instruct the Provincial Accountant to review the reported balance in the Due
to NGAs account to reflect the utilization and liquidation for the year.

8.9 We recommended that the Provincial Accountant (a) coordinate with the
Provincial Treasurer to identify the details of the noted discrepancy between
the reciprocal accounts; (b) immediately effect the adjustments upon
identification thereof; and (c) ensure that the reciprocal accounts are
reconciled at all times in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated
December 1, 2015.

9.28 We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the:
a) PDRRMO to: (i) Review and ensure the completeness of the contents of
the LDRRMFIP before its submission to the LCE for approval;
(ii) Coordinate with the Provincial Planning and Development Office
(PPDO) and other concerned department of the Provincial Government
and conduct investigation, survey or any other appropriate assessment
of the unimplemented DRRM PPAs, evaluate the feasibility of
implementation and facilitate the realignment of the funds to other
priority PPAs, if not implementable during the year; (iii) Incorporate in
74
the succeeding year’s LDRRMFIP those projects that are ready for
implementation indicating the specific and realistic timeframe; and (iv)
Strictly monitor the implementation/completion of the DRRM PPAs
according to plans and targets;
b) PGSO to designate a full time personnel to properly handle the records
of all the procured supplies, materials and equipment pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2014-002, preparation of monthly Report of Supplies and
Materials Issued (RSMI) based on RIS and inventory list of all procured
and donated items based on the Stock Cards and Property Cards which
shall be submitted to Accounting Unit as basis in recording thereof in the
books of accounts; and
c) Provincial Accountant to (i) Re-evaluate the designation and assignment
of accounting personnel and ensure that respective duties and functions
are properly performed particularly on the preparation and updating of
PPE and Supplies Ledger Cards for procured supplies, materials and
equipment; (ii) revert the unutilized balance of LDRRM Fund for CYs
2014-2015 totaling P113,258.88 as required under Item 5.1.13 of COA
Circular No. 2012-002; (iii) ensure that the details of unexpended balance
of LDRRMF for the current and previous years are properly disclosed
in the Notes to Financial Statements; (iv) require the Special Disbursing
Officer to submit written explanation and documents for allowing
payments of cash assistance to individuals other than the targeted
beneficiaries without proper authorization ; and (v) submit duly
approved Reports of Waste Materials to support the procurement of
materials and supplies totaling P456,474.00.
9.29 We likewise recommended that the Provincial Governor designate a
personnel to create and maintain an official website of the PGR wherein
reports on appropriation, receipts and utilization of LDRRMF and other
funds will be posted in adherence to the transparency governing principle of
good governance pursuant to Section 3(f), Rule 1 of IRR of R.A. No. 10121
also known as the PDRRM Act of 2010.

10.13 We recommended that the Provincial Development Council (PDC) through
the Provincial Planning and Development Office and Provincial Engineering
Office:
a. Submit complete and detailed reasons/explanations for the non-
implementation of the 89 development PPAs to enable the Audit
Team to recommend a doable measure to facilitate implementation

b. Conduct investigation, survey or any other appropriate assessment

indicating the specific and realistic timeframe for its
implementation; and
d. Strictly monitor the schedules of implementation of the
79
development PPAs of the PGR and ensure completion thereof
strictly in accordance within its target.

Budget for the Contracts (ABCs) of the 46 development infrastructure
contracts.

observations.recommendation:
With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records and henceforth comply with Sections 12, 30, 31 and 32 of GAM for LGUs in preparing the SL and Cashbook. Moreover, enjoin the Municipal Accountant to consider the PAJE relative to the book reconciling items and present fairly the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements.

Further, instruct the Accountant to make sure that the bank statements are stamped as received and indicated with date of receipt thereof. Finally, should there be bank accounts that to date have no copy yet of monthly bank statements, send a letter to the depository banks requesting copies therefor.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the BAC to provide to the Audit Team the noted documentary deficiencies in the procurement process and payments to suppliers and/or justification thereof; (b) explain and/or justify the use of cash advance in paying suppliers and the use of reimbursement basis in procuring goods; and               (c) require the Municipal Accountant to consider the PAJE and ensure henceforth that the payments to suppliers are supported with complete documents.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to consider the PAJE in order to correct the misstatement and present fairly the financial performance of the municipality; and (b) require the Disbursing Officer to substantiate the payout made to the above-named persons and/or explain/justify the above-noted findings. Otherwise, produce and refund to the local treasury the amount deemed to have not been paid to the beneficiaries.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to (a) reconcile the accounts Due to NGAs and Trust Liability – DRRMF; (b) submit the updated SL and Report on Fund Utilization of the BGCM; (c) substantiate the eight adjusting journal entries in Annex H with supporting documents, schedules, computation and analysis and submit the same to the audit team; and (d) consider the PAJE in order to present fairly the Due to NGAs account in the FS.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) direct the Municipal Engineer and BAC Chairperson to submit the noted lacking documents for the infrastructure projects listed in Annex I; and (b) the Municipal Accountant to i) impose the correct amount of liquidated damages against the contractors of the projects listed in Annex J; ii) henceforth, ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior to processing for payments, the accuracy of the STE in the computation of the number of days of delay and the amount of liquidated damages; and iii) consider the PAJE in order to correct the account balances.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal Accountant (a) refrain from granting cash advance to those officers and employees with outstanding balance; (b) enforce the immediate settlement or liquidation of all outstanding cash advances particularly for those aged more than 30 days to more than five years; (c) employ internal controls such as issuance of memorandum or demand letter to delinquent officers and employees who have accounts that already due on bordering the deadline of 30-days; and (d) reconcile the balances reported in the FS, SL and RUCA and ensure always that these reports are fairly presented.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the MGSO and the Municipal Accountant to comply with the process in storing, release, and recording of inventories as provided for in Section 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to update his records and ensure henceforth that all inventories are properly accounted.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Engineer to estimate the useful life of the infrastructure assets and the Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for depreciation thereof; (b) enjoin the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile thoroughly the results of the physical count with the accounting records; (c) require the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Assessor (for the land account) to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs and submit the RPCPPE on time; and (d) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare SL for all PPE accounts and reconcile them with the GL and FS.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

enjoin the Disbursing Officer/s to submit immediately to the Audit Team their breakdown of recipients/beneficiaries per payroll in order to reconcile the difference and explain in writing the above-noted difference of P5,000.00;
require the Municipal Accountant to explain the understated total refund, overstated net disbursements, and understated amount refundable in the FUR and instruct the immediate submission of revised FUR to the DSWD MIMAROPA in order to refund the remaining balance of P90,000.00; and
instruct the MSWDO and the Disbursing Officers to submit to the Audit Team copies of photos during payout of cash assistance to the two above-named recipients/beneficiaries who now said that they have not received such assistance.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) and the Municipal Budget Officer (a) revert the salary rates to previous monthly basic salary in order to reduce the PS cost and comply with the PS limitation requirement; and (b) direct the refund of the salary increase (net of withholdings made).

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:

the BAC to submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team and henceforth take note of the additional observations and adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in all stages of procurement process and ensure that all bid documents are complete, evaluated, and submitted on time; and
the BAC and contractor to use the prescribed bid form and contract agreement in the 5th edition of PBD issued by the GPPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) direct the LDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to submit a revised Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and henceforth adequately prepare and submit the said report in the prescribed format required under COA Circular No. 2012-002; and (b) require the Municipal Accountant to consider the PAJE, transfer the unexpended/unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE to special trust fund and disclose the same in the Notes to FS including those pertaining to prior years in compliance with Item 5.1.16 and Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to limit the payroll deductions following the order of preference provided for in the GAA and henceforth ensure that the employee's net take home pay be not less than the mandatory minimum amount.

observations.recommendation:
With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
(a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the FS and GL, prepare a
comprehensive list of reconciling items, and record them in the books in
order to present fairly the account balance as at year-end; (b) require the
Municipal Treasurer to update regularly the Cashbooks and reconcile them
with the GL; (c) enjoin the depository banks to furnish the municipality of
bank statements on time; (d) see to it that BRS are prepared and submitted
by the Accounting Office on time; and (d) ensure that all bank accounts with
the private banks are closed and/or transferred to AGDBs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (a) record the whole amount of IRA received in the Share
from IRA account under the General Fund; (b) draw the necessary adjusting
entry for the noted misstatement; and (c) observe properly the correct usage
of intra-agency accounts and make sure that they are always equal.

The management has not yet replied to the issued AOM.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (a) record under Due to NGAs account the receipt, liquidation
and refund of funds received from NGAs; (b) consider the proposed
adjusting journal entry (PAJE) amounting to P3,401,000.00 and P58,878.70
in adjusting the asset and liability accounts; and (c) make sure henceforth
that the JEVs are detailed and supported with complete documents in order
to identify valid fund transfers.

In a letter reply dated July 7, 2020, the Municipal Accountant agreed to
adjust the account balance by drawing JEV dated March 30, 2020. However,
instead of following the PAJE, he drawn the following entry below:
Account Debit Credit
1) Government Equity P3,401,000.00
Due to NGAs P3,401,000.00
To record adjusting entry
Total P3,401,000.00 P3,401,000.00
He posited that he could not debit Cash in Bank account because the funds
were actually transferred to a bank account under the Trust Fund.
AuditorRejoinder
We acknowledge that the Municipal Accountant is willing to adjust the
2020 account balances. However, we would like to underscore that while the fund
was actually transferred to a bank account under the Trust Fund, no such journal
entry was recorded to reflect the transfer. Hence, the need to recognize the Cash
in Bank account under the Trust Fund, instead of a debit to equity account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (a) reclassify from Due to Special Accounts to Loans Payable -
Domestic account the amount of P5,423,820.86 representing the unadjusted
loan balance of the municipality in compliance with PPSAS 1 and COA
Circular No. 2015-009; and (b) make the necessary adjusting entries to
reflect the payments made for the quarterly amortization P903,970.15.

The Municipal Accountant in his letter dated August 23, 2019 commented
that necessary corrections will be made in the books, however, the same
observation was still noted as at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) enjoin the LDRRMO
to justify/explain the low fund utilization rate caused by non-implementation
of PPAs and ensure henceforth that the PPAs for disaster prevention and
mitigation, response, rehabilitation and recovery be implemented and the
funds therefor be utilized for the benefits of the constituents; and (b) require
the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended/unutilized QRF and
MF-MOOE to special trust fund and disclose the same in the Notes to FS
including those pertaining to prior years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (a) ensure that the transfer of unutilized/unexpended DRRMF
is recorded on time by debiting the Transfer of Unspent Current Year
DRRMF to the Trust Fund (5-02-15-010); (b) consider the presented PAJE in
order to correct the Due from Other Funds account balance and present the
affected accounts fairly in the financial statements; and (c) reconcile the
reciprocal accounts of Due from/to Other Funds.

The Municipal Accountant in his letter dated July 7, 2020 commented that
the discrepancy in the balances of reciprocal accounts was due "[i]n part on
[their] unawareness [of] the proper accounting entries to be made". He also
said that he is willing to prepare adjusting entry to rectify the incorrect balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to furnish immediately the Audit Team of the required Cashbook
and rectify the current Cashbook (Cash in Treasury) by providing a column
for collections and henceforth ensure that it is updated daily and reconciled
with the GL.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the
Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies in intensifying the collection of real
property tax such as information campaign to encourage taxpayers to take
advantage of early payment discount; (b) require the Municipal Treasurer
and Accountant to reconcile their records and present the correct amount of
tax delinquencies; (c) require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the
year-end balances of RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred RPT and SET
accounts and adjust the GL and FS for the unrecorded collections; and
(d) require the Municipal Assessor to coordinate with the Provincial Assessor
in the conduct of tax mapping to update the real property units and their
corresponding assessed value within the municipality.

In a letter dated July 7, 2020, the Municipal Assessor explained that the
municipality really needs to be updated when it comes to tax mapping, however,
due to limited number of personnel and the lack of technical people who can do
the job, they are completely dependent on the records of the Office of the
Provincial Assessor in this particular area.
Moreover, the Municipal Accountant also replied on the same date that
adjustments are made in CY 2020 to reflect in the books the accurate amount of
RPT and SET Receivables.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the
Municipal Accountant to process only the request for cash advance of the
officers and employees who have no outstanding balance; (b) require the
officers and employees to liquidate immediately their outstanding balances;
and (c) establish internal control in order to avoid late liquidation which
should include issuance of written notice to persons with cash advances
already bordering the deadline and with cash advances already due.

In a letter dated July 6, 2020, it was disclosed that the "[A]ccounting
Office has already recommended xxx the suspension of salaries of xxx officials
and employees with unliquidated cash advances". Also, the Municipal Accountant
said that they "[r]egularly furnished the concerned officials and employees with
unliquidated cash advances with a letter reminding them to liquidate". However,
the Audit Team was not furnished with copies of the said letter for validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require (a) the
Municipal Accountant to reconcile his SL, GL and FS and ensure faithful
presentation thereof; (b) the MGSO and the Municipal Accountant to
maintain Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Card, respectively, in order for
the former to have point of reference in the conduct of physical count and the
latter to determine the correct account balances at year-end; and
(c) determine the existence and usability of the noted non-moving inventory
accounts and adjust the same if proven to be nonexistent.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
(a) require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the completed PPEs from
the Trust Fund to the General Fund and provide depreciation allowance for
all depreciable PPEs in the books; (b) instruct the Municipal Engineer to
determine the useful life of all infrastructure assets in order to compute
depreciation expenses; (c) direct the MGSO to conduct a complete physical
count of all PPEs, accomplish properly the RPCPEE and submit it on time;
and (d) require the Municipal Accountant to make sure that the SL, GL and
FS are updated and reconciled.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the
Municipal Accountant to monitor the withholdings and remittances of taxes
by diligently preparing the SL per type of tax in compliance with Section 10
of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant
to accurately and completely record the transactions in the GL and consider
the PAJE of the Audit Team.

In a letter-reply dated July 8, 2020, the Municipal Accountant commented
that the withheld taxes were remitted in a timely manner and in accordance with
the prescribed period. Moreover, he asserted that the agency was not negligent in
its mandated obligation of remitting various taxes withheld to the BIR thru
electronic Payment and Filing System (eFPS). To further prove their claim, the
Accounting Office submitted various disbursement vouchers (DVs) and
withholding tax returns for the CY 2019.
AuditorRejoinder
While we also acknowledge the efforts of the Accounting Office in
making sure that remittances are made to the BIR, The Audit Team stands by its
foregoing observation because our recomputation still resulted in under
remittance, primarily due to huge forwarded beginning balance that remained
unremitted to the BIR.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the AAO to
submit an ARA to the GSIS in order for the latter to update its MSP and
Billing Statement. Also, direct the AAO and the Electronic Remittance File
(ERF) Handler in the Accounting Office to conduct reconciliation regularly
between the actual amount of contributions and shares in the payroll versus
the amount being billed by the GSIS in order to trace the root cause of
under/over remittance.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor thru
the Municipal Budget Officer (a) strictly observe the limitation over the PS
cost set forth under Section 325(a) of RA No. 7160 and Section 6.3.6 of LBC
No. 98; (b) refrain from providing additional PS expenditures until such time
that the PS cap is observed; and (c) require all officers and employees to
refund the SRI for CY 2019 and other personal benefits granted to them in
excess of the actual PS limitation.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer require the contractor
to install and/or accomplish the items found to be non-existent in the projects
and submit the noted lacking documents to the Audit Team for further
evaluation. Then, apprise the Audit Team of any installation made in
compliance with this audit recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the
Municipal Engineer to explain/justify the higher ABC and contract amount
over the cost estimate per audit for the project Design, Build and Supply
of -20oC Cold Storage Facilities and Equipment and to submit to the Audit
Team for further evaluation the Detailed Breakdown of ABC, complete set of
plans and drawings, variation order and design specifications for the 67.62%
of the total works and the documents supporting the request for time
extension; (b) require the BAC to explain/justify the observations on
quorum, arithmetical error, similar amounts and the same date of POW
preparation and bidding activity; and (c) explain/justify why the project has
not been utilized and non-operational for years since its completion and
apprise the Audit Team of its current operational status.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to
justify the post-qualification adjudged to the LCB despite the noted
discrepancies and deficiencies in the submitted bidding documents. Also,
require the BAC to provide the noted lacking documents and henceforth
ensure that the contracts are perfected and submitted on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the
Municipal Engineer to submit the lacking documents for the completed
projects and ensure strict compliance with Section 62 of the 2016 Revised
IRR of Republic Act No. 9184; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant
that henceforth, each voucher must have complete supporting documents
prior to processing of claims in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001
and Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the MPDC
and Municipal Engineer to implement the developmental projects within the
target period as incorporated in the AIP; and (b) disclose all government
infrastructure projects in the quarterly Report of Government PPAs and
submit the same to the Audit Team on time in compliance with Item 2 of
COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GFPS
Focal Person to (a) ensure henceforth that the GAD objectives, activities,
targets and performance indicators are well articulated and comprehensive
in order to secure an endorsement from the PPDO and DILG; and
(b) refrain from inputting the actual amount of expenditures as already the
attributed amount but rather determine first the equivalent percentage of
the HGDG PIMME score to compute the allowed amount of attribution.
The Management commented that they will comply with the audit
recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, who also heads the
Municipal Solid Waste Board, to (a) follow up the approval of the NSWMC
to the municipalitys 10-Year SWM Plan; (b) secure ECC from DENR for
the waste disposal site; and (c) undertake necessary actions to prevent the
open dumpsite from further becoming an uncontrolled open dumpsite.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Treasurer to submit all monthly and quarterly financial
and accounting reports and their corresponding statements within the
prescribed period in order for the Audit Team to undertake timely review
and post-audit of the transactions and accounts and at the same time to
inform management of any defects or deficiencies.

send demand letter to those who failed to liquidate after the lapse of the
prescribed period in compliance with Item V.2 of COA Memorandum
No. 2017-010; and (c) the Municipal Accountant submit immediately copies
of LRs and JEVs for the reported liquidation listed in Annex E in compliance
with Item 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) require the conduct of complete physical count and the reconciliation of
accounting records with the RPCPPE and RPCI pursuant to Section 124 of
the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1; (b) substantiate the issuance
of inventories totaling P97,200.00 and require the Municipal Accountant to
draw the necessary adjusting entries to reflect in the books the correct
balance of inventory; and (c) instruct the MGSO to strengthen its internal
control over municipal properties by putting property tags and securing
PARs.

(a) the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to return the unexpended funds
totalling P216,148.41 to its respective source agencies and secure official
receipt for the same in compliance with Item Nos. 4.9 and 5.5 of COA
Circular No. 94-013; and (b) the Municipal Accountant to prepare SLs for all
funds transferred and make sure that the Due to NGAs account is debited
only upon project completion and full liquidation to source agency in
compliance with Item 4.5 and Annex A of COA Circular No. 94-013 and
COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to reconcile the disparity between the balances of the reciprocal
accounts Due from other funds and Due to other funds and present the
correct balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to maintain a complete and detailed Subsidiary Ledger for all
real accounts to facilitate the analysis and evaluation of the affected General
Ledger balances in compliance with Section 10 of the Manual on the NGAS
for LGUs, Volume II and Section 111 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to comply with DOF Department Order No. 27-05 and
Department Circular No. 001-2015 relative to a sound and efficient
management of government funds by transferring all accounts from private
bank to AGDB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer and other concerned officials and employees to study and consider
the adoption and shifting to automated payroll system in partnership with
the AGDBs to ensure stronger internal control and efficient processing of
payroll funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to limit the payroll deductions following the order of preference
provided for in the GAA and henceforth ensure that the employee's net take
home pay be not less than the mandatory minimum amount.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
ensure that: (a) the Municipal Budget Officer strictly observe the general
limitations on personal services cited under Section 325(a) of RA No. 7160
and Section 6.3.6 of LBC No. 98 and to refrain from providing additional PS
expenditures until such time that the PS cap is observed; and (b) require all
officers and employees to refund the PBB for CY 2017 and other personal
services/benefits granted to them in excess of the PS limitation.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) require the BAC to submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team
and henceforth adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of
R.A. No. 9184 in all stages of procurement process and ensure that all bid
documents are complete, evaluated and submitted on time; and (b) instruct
the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness
and veracity of supporting documents prior to processing of claims for
payment of the above infrastructure projects in compliance with COA
Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

henceforth adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A.
No. 9184 in all stages of procurement process and ensure that all bid
documents are complete and evaluated; and (b) instruct the Municipal
Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness and veracity of
supporting documents prior to processing of claims for payment of purchases
in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of P.D.
No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer: (a) require the
contractor to repair the Fish Port pursuant to Section 62 of the 2016 Revised
IRR of R.A. No. 9184; (b) submit the actual PERT/CPM/S-Curve in order
for the Municipal Accountant to determine and impose applicable liquidated
damages pursuant to Section 68 of the same 2016 Revised IRR;
(c) explain/justify the higher ABC and contract amount over the cost
estimate per audit for the project Cobrador Water System, otherwise, refund
the noted variance of P167,818.85; (d) require the installment of the items
found to be non-existent in the project Cobrador Water System; and
(e) update the Audit Team whether the Cobrador Water System is already
operational by submitting a status report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the
amount and details of previous years' unexpended balance of LDRRMF in
compliance with Item 5.1.16 and Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor fast track the
implementation of identified developmental PPAs and maximize the
utilization of 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an appropriate
policy directive to implement all PPAs provided for in the GPB and mandate
the GFPS to monitor its implementation in compliance with Item 4.1.C of the
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01. Moreover, the GFPS TWG
shall make an effort to increase the gender responsiveness of the PPAs
attributed to the GAD Fund and report the accurate attribution to the GAD
budgets in compliance with the same JMC.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, who also heads the
Municipal Solid Waste Board, to: (a) follow up the approval of the NSWMC
to the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan transmitted by the
municipality; (b) encourage active participation of the 31 barangays to
support the SWM program of the municipality by educating their respective
constituents through Information Education Campaign on the proper
segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes and
the importance of their participation in the implementation of SWM
programs; and (c) ensure that the MRFs of barangays are functional and the
purpose of establishing municipal MRF and sanitary landfill will be served.
